name: Lotka-Volterra predator-prey equations
principles: mass-action law applied to ecological encounters
exponential intrinsic growth / decay with bilinear coupling
formulaTeX: \dot{x} = \alpha\, x \;-\; \beta\, x\, y, \qquad \dot{y} = \delta\, x\, y \;-\; \gamma\, y
conventions: x(t) is the prey population; y(t) is the predator population
alpha > 0 is the prey intrinsic growth rate (per capita, in absence of predators)
beta > 0 is the predation rate (rate of prey loss per predator-prey encounter)
delta > 0 is the predator's per-capita gain from consuming prey (efficiency-times-encounter-rate)
gamma > 0 is the predator intrinsic death rate (per capita, in absence of prey)
well-mixed assumption: spatial structure ignored; populations treated as continuous variables (large-N limit)
expectedLimits: y = 0 (no predators): dot x = alpha x — pure Malthusian exponential prey growth
x = 0 (no prey): dot y = -gamma y — exponential predator decay to extinction
fixed points: trivial (0, 0) — unstable saddle; coexistence (gamma/delta, alpha/beta) — neutrally stable centre
trajectories around the coexistence fixed point are closed orbits (conserved quantity: V(x, y) = delta x - gamma ln x + beta y - alpha ln y)
small perturbations from the fixed point: linearised oscillations with angular frequency omega = sqrt(alpha gamma)
limit-cycle behaviour fragile: any logistic / saturation correction (carrying capacity, functional response) destroys the closed orbits and replaces them with damped or limit-cycle dynamics
stochastic limit (small populations): demographic noise can drive extinction of one species, breaking the deterministic picture
references: Volterra (1926) — Variazioni e fluttuazioni del numero d'individui in specie animali conviventi, Memorie della Reale Accademia dei Lincei
Murray — Mathematical Biology I: An Introduction, ch.3
Edelstein-Keshet — Mathematical Models in Biology, ch.6
